preamble.summary: The final Song of Ascents. In just three verses, the psalm calls on the servants of the LORD who stand in the temple at night to lift their hands and bless God, then pronounces a blessing from Zion back upon the worshiper. It is both the conclusion of the pilgrimage collection and a liturgical exchange between the arriving pilgrims and the temple ministers.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 134 closes the Songs of Ascent (120-134) with an exchange of blessing. The pilgrims have arrived. The journey that began in Psalm 120 with distress among hostile neighbors ends here in the temple courts at night. The psalm's structure is a call and response: verses 1-2 are the pilgrims' call to the night-shift priests and Levites ('Bless the LORD!'), and verse 3 is the priests' answering benediction ('May the LORD bless you from Zion'). Blessing flows in both directions — upward from people to God and downward from God through the priests to the people. The brevity is itself meaningful: after fifteen psalms of journey, longing, trust, and arrival, the final word is simply 'bless' — repeated three times in three verses.
preamble.friction: The phrase omdim be-veit YHWH ba-leilot ('standing in the house of the LORD at night') raises questions about nighttime temple worship. While the regular sacrificial schedule was centered on morning and evening, 1 Chronicles 9:33 mentions Levitical singers who served 'day and night,' and Psalm 42:8 speaks of God's song 'in the night.' The night watch was a real feature of temple life, not merely poetic atmosphere. The night setting also creates theological resonance: even in darkness, the servants of the LORD stand and bless.
preamble.connections: As the final Song of Ascents, Psalm 134 echoes and resolves themes from the entire collection. The 'lifting of hands' (se'u yedeikhem) toward the sanctuary recalls Psalm 121:1 ('I lift my eyes to the hills'). The blessing from Zion echoes Psalm 128:5 ('May the LORD bless you from Zion'). The title oseh shamayim va-arets ('maker of heaven and earth') appeared in Psalm 121:2 and Psalm 124:8, forming an inclusio that brackets the entire collection. The pilgrimage that began with a cry for help from the maker of heaven and earth ends with a blessing from the same maker.

verses.text_hebrew:
שִׁ֗יר הַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת
הִנֵּ֤ה בָּרְכ֣וּ אֶת־יְ֭הוָה כׇּל־עַבְדֵ֣י יְהוָ֑ה
הָעֹמְדִ֥ים בְּבֵית־יְ֝הוָ֗ה בַּלֵּילֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, bless ye the LORD, all ye servants of the LORD, which by night stand in the house of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents.
Come, bless the LORD,
all you servants of the LORD
who stand in the house of the LORD at night.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with hinneh ('behold, come, look!') followed by an imperative: barekhu et YHWH ('bless the LORD'). The call is addressed to kol avdei YHWH ('all servants of the LORD') — the priests and Levites who minister in the temple. They are further described as ha-omdim be-veit YHWH ba-leilot ('the ones standing in the house of the LORD in the nights'). The plural ba-leilot ('in the nights,' not 'at night') suggests this is a regular, ongoing practice — night after night, these servants stand watch in God's house. The arriving pilgrims, having completed their ascent, now call upon the permanent temple staff to do what they do: bless the LORD.
translator_notes: The phrase avdei YHWH ('servants of the LORD') can refer broadly to all worshipers or specifically to the Levitical temple staff. In this context, the night setting and the standing in the house of the LORD point to the temple ministers. The verb omdim ('standing') is a technical term for priestly service — to 'stand before the LORD' means to serve in an official capacity (Deuteronomy 10:8).

verses.text_kjv: Lift up your hands in the sanctuary, and bless the LORD.
verses.rendering:
Lift your hands toward the sanctuary
and bless the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The command intensifies: se'u yedeikhem qodesh ('lift your hands toward holiness/the holy place'). The word qodesh could mean 'in holiness' (describing the manner of lifting) or 'toward the sanctuary' (describing the direction). Both readings work: the hands are lifted in a holy manner toward the holy place. Lifting hands is the posture of prayer and blessing throughout the Hebrew Bible — open palms raised toward heaven, receiving and offering simultaneously. The repetition of barekhu et YHWH ('bless the LORD') from verse 1 completes the call: stand, lift, bless.
translator_notes: The ambiguity of qodesh ('holiness' or 'the holy place/sanctuary') is likely intentional. The lifted hands are directed toward the sacred space (the inner sanctuary where God's presence dwells) and the act itself is sacred (performed in holiness). Hebrew poets often exploit the double meaning of spatial and qualitative terms.

verses.text_hebrew:
יְבָרֶכְךָ֣ יְ֭הוָה מִצִּיּ֑וֹן
עֹ֝שֵׂ֗ה שָׁמַ֥יִם וָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD that made heaven and earth bless thee out of Zion.
verses.rendering:
May the LORD bless you from Zion —
the maker of heaven and earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The voice shifts. The pilgrims called on the priests to bless God; now the priests respond with a blessing on the pilgrims. Yevarekekha YHWH mi-Tsiyon ('May the LORD bless you from Zion') sends the pilgrim home carrying Zion's blessing. The singular 'you' (the suffix -kha) individualizes the benediction — each pilgrim is personally blessed. The final title, oseh shamayim va-arets ('maker of heaven and earth'), is the grandest possible identification of the God who blesses. The one who made everything blesses from this particular place. The Songs of Ascent opened in Psalm 121:2 with 'my help comes from the LORD, maker of heaven and earth' and now close with the same title. The journey is complete: the pilgrim who trusted the maker has arrived and received the maker's blessing.
translator_notes: The phrase oseh shamayim va-arets ('maker of heaven and earth') forms an inclusio with Psalm 121:2 and Psalm 124:8, bracketing the Songs of Ascent. The God who helped the pilgrim start the journey is the same God who blesses at the destination. This structural repetition is deliberate and marks the collection as a unified literary whole.

The shift from plural address in verses 1-2 (barekhu, 'bless,' plural) to singular in verse 3 (yevarekekha, 'may he bless you,' singular) may reflect a liturgical practice: the congregation calls out together, and the priest pronounces the blessing on each individual worshiper.
